hero.alt: Project Simile — Meta Account migration for Reality Labs wearables.
hero.caption: Project Simile — migrating every Reality Labs device user onto a Meta account.

brief: Project Simile migrated every device user across Reality Labs onto Meta accounts, removing the long-standing Facebook requirement for Ray-Ban Stories and the next-gen glasses. As sole design lead, I coordinated Legal, Privacy, Comms, PM, Design Systems, Content Design, and Engineering across the full go-to-market and launch — operating for over a year through shifting legal, technical, and regulatory requirements.

overviewMedia.alt: What is Project Simile — overview of the Meta account value proposition.
overviewMedia.caption: A Meta account gives users more control over their identity, better shared experiences across the Family of Apps, and the freedom to use email and password to access Reality Labs devices.

body.heading: What is Project Simile?
body.body: Project Simile migrates all device users across Reality Labs onto Meta accounts. At a high level, a Meta account gives users more flexibility and control over how they manage their identity, better shared experiences across the Family of Apps, and the freedom to choose an email and password to access Reality Labs devices — instead of being forced to use a Facebook account.

media.alt: Project Simile value proposition: identity control, shared experiences, and email/password sign-in.

body.heading: My role
body.body:
Sole design lead coordinating Legal, Privacy, Comms, PM, Design Systems, Content Design, and Engineering across the full go-to-market and launch.

Strategic influence: proactively inserted Wearables into the company-wide "Future 3" workstream, ensuring the wearables voice was at the table early rather than as an afterthought. Ambiguity navigation: operated for over a year through legal hurdles, technical challenges, and shifting requirements. Proactive risk mitigation: de-risked the next-gen glasses months ahead by documenting edge cases and driving resolution before they became blockers.

media.alt: My role on Project Simile — cross-functional design leadership across Legal, Privacy, PM, and Engineering.

body.heading: Why users wanted this
body.body:
An app-store analysis identified that 28% of negative user reviews originated from being forced to use a Facebook account to use Ray-Ban Stories. Users were frustrated with the requirement — one actual review read: "it makes you create a Facebook account. This is unnecessary for the functionality."

Removing the Facebook requirement wasn't just an identity-platform migration — it directly addressed the single biggest source of negative sentiment for the product.

media.alt: App-store review analysis — 28% of negative reviews were about the Facebook requirement.

body.heading: Launch timeline
body.body: The migration rolled out in stages across 2023. Existing glasses users were notified of the upcoming Meta account migration in May 2023. Meta accounts launched for all users in June 2023, with existing users migrating from their Facebook account and new users logging in or creating a Meta account directly. By October 2023, Meta login applied to the second-generation glasses.

media.alt: Smart glasses launch timeline for the Meta account migration across 2023.

body.heading: Partnership with VR
body.body: Project Simile spanned both Smart Glasses and VR. For launch, we agreed to let the experiences diverge where appropriate — VR was mid-A/B-experimentation with no settled optimal flow, Smart Glasses was building on different (Native Bloks) technology, and there were known issues in the VR experience that Smart Glasses could improve on. Post-launch, the plan was to unify: VR would adopt the Native Bloks technology built by Smart Glasses, and we'd improve the combined experience with everything learned from both launches.

media.alt: Smart Glasses and VR partnership — diverge for launch, unify afterward.

body.heading: Existing users — happy path
body.body:
For an existing user migrating immediately: we notify them of the upcoming migration in advance, they open the app, get educated on the change, create or log into a Meta account, update firmware if required, then turn on and connect their glasses — ready to use.

The migration flow was designed so users always understood what was changing at each step, never lost access to their imported media, and could resolve issues without contacting support.

body.heading: Delayed migration & forced reset
body.body:
Not everyone migrates immediately, so the system had to absorb delay gracefully. Users could postpone migration by a set number of days, with active notifications — push, email, and in-app modals — making it clear they had a limited window before risking a factory reset. Crucially, users could still access their already-imported media in the Gallery while postponing.

If a user kept delaying (or an inactive user opened the app afterward), they'd eventually have to factory reset their glasses — which would erase any unimported captures. Designing this edge case honestly, with repeated forewarning, was what let Legal and Trust ship the migration without a punitive feel.

media.alt: Delayed migration flow — postpone with active notifications.
media.caption: Delayed migration: users can postpone, but are actively reminded before the window closes.

media.alt: Forced factory reset edge case after repeated delays.
media.caption: The forced-reset edge case, designed with repeated forewarning so it never feels punitive.

body.heading: Value proposition & legal
body.body: At launch we had a deliberately basic value prop until further functionality could be built into Account Center — we expected users to still have questions about how a Meta account benefits them. Regulators required that setting up with Facebook and with email be presented equally, so the create-account flow gave both options the same weight. Migrating users wouldn't see an option to create a Meta account with Instagram, but could always link Instagram later in Account Center.

media.alt: Meta account value proposition and regulator-driven equal presentation of sign-up options.

body.heading: Design systems
body.body: The migration spanned three different design systems, and part of the work was making a coherent experience across all of them. Project Simile experiences lived in the X-Meta Design System (gradient backgrounds, blue buttons); Smart Glasses experiences lived in the Ray-Ban Stories Design System (white background, black buttons); and the new app introduced a dark-mode-only, gradient-forward system. Reconciling the visual language across these handoffs — so a single migration never felt like three different products — was a core part of the craft.

media.alt: Three design systems reconciled across the migration: X-Meta DS, Ray-Ban Stories DS, and the new app DS.

body.heading: New users — happy path
body.body: For brand-new users, the flow folded migration into the out-of-box experience: open Ray-Ban Stories and download the app, get started, land on the login screen, create or log into a Meta account, run any required firmware update, then continue through pairing and the rest of OOBE. Edge cases — like an existing user who hadn't finished migration arriving through the new-user path — were mapped explicitly so no one got stuck between the two flows.

media.alt: New users happy-path flow with migration folded into onboarding.

body.heading: Impact & outcome
body.body: Successfully launched Meta accounts for all smart glasses users — eliminating the #1 negative feedback theme from app-store reviews. The migration was experienced as quick and frictionless: users understood what was changing and why, logged in fast, and could resolve issues painlessly. And we navigated heavy legal and regulatory scrutiny of the privacy-policy updates without negative press backlash.

media.alt: Impact and outcome — eliminated the top negative review theme, frictionless migration, no regulatory backlash.
